figures.caption: Figure 3: Detailed results across Counselor- and Client-Level therapy metrics
Table 1: Main results in terms of counselor- and client-level metrics. † means the model is based on Qwen3-8B. Best results are in bold and second-best results are underlined.

Figure 1: The overall architecture of PsychAgent, an experience-driven lifelong learning framework for psychological counseling. The system operates through a synergistic closed-loop mechanism: (a) The Memory-Augmented Planning Engine ensures therapeutic continuity across longitudinal sessions by reasoning over dynamic client profiles and formulating strategic goals; (b) The Skill Evolution Engine manages a hierarchical repository of therapeutic techniques, supporting both real-time context-aware retrieval and the post-session abstraction of novel skills; (c) The Reinforced Internalization Engine solidifies these capabilities via rejection fine-tuning, where optimal trajectories are selected to transform explicit skills into the model’s endogenous intuition.
Table 6: Systematic mean-score bias of LLM Rater (Gemini-3) relative to each human rater. Positive values indicate that the LLM assigns higher scores on average.

Table 9: Representative functional families reused across therapies after internalization. The main evidence comes from family-level reuse rather than from frequent exact mixed-therapy bundles in a single turn.
Table 5: Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) across raters.

Table 8: Representative candidate functional skills after internalization. These packages are best interpreted as operationally new functional units built from known micro-skills, rather than as entirely new canonical techniques.
